mnemonics.title: Raspberry Pi Power Checklist
mnemonics.content: P-I: Power Input 5V - Always use 5V power supply to avoid damage.

mnemonics.title: GPIO Remember
mnemonics.content: GPIO = General Purpose Input/Output - your bridge to the physical world in IoT projects.

mnemonics.title: Pi Model Evolution
mnemonics.content: Pi 1 → Pi 2 → Pi 3 → Pi 4: Each doubled cores and increased RAM capability.

acronyms.usage: Complete computer on one board like Raspberry Pi
Pins for connecting sensors and actuators
Integrated processor with CPU, GPU, memory on one chip
Processor architecture used by Raspberry Pi

visualTips.visualization: Visualize the Raspberry Pi board as a mini city: CPU (city hall) in center, GPIO (main gate) on edge, USB/HDMI (entrances) on sides, microSD (storage district) at bottom.
Think of GPIO pins as addresses on a street - each has a unique number (GPIO 2, GPIO 3, etc.) for sending commands to devices.
Power enters like water through a pipe (micro-USB), flows through the board (SoC), and distributes to all components - too little pressure (voltage) causes brownouts.

analogies.analogy: Just as a brain processes information and controls body functions, the SoC processes data and controls all components of the Raspberry Pi board.
GPIO pins are like nerves - they carry electrical signals (information) between the Pi's 'brain' and external sensors/actuators in the physical world.
The microSD card is like the brain's long-term memory - it stores the operating system, programs, and data persistently.
USB ports are like hands - they let the Raspberry Pi connect and interact with external devices (keyboard, mouse, sensors).
